content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

May 16, 1990

Wayne Butterbaugh
Palos Verdes Peninsula
Unified School District
3801 Via La Selva
Palos Verdes Estates, CA 90274

Re: Your District's Conflict of Interest Code
Our File No. I-90-225

Dear Mr. Butterbaugh:

It has been brought to our attention that the conflict of
interest code for the Palos Verdes Peninsula Unified School
District may be out-of-date and in need of revision. We have had
an opportunity to review the code and have found some of its
provisions are not consistent with the provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act").1/ We hope this letter will assist you in
updating your code.

The Act at Section 87300 requires each governmental agency to
have a conflict of interest code. Section 87302 contains the
provisions that are required to be in codes. In 1980, the
Commission adopted a regulation which contains those provisions
required by Section 87302. (Regulation 18730.) For instance, the
regulation spells out the reporting thresholds, the manner of
disqualification and the manner in which interests are to be
disclosed. By incorporating Regulation 18730 into your code, the
provisions of your code will always be up-to-date. You will only
need to update the appendix portion of your code when any
personnel changes occur within the district. For agencies for
which we are the code reviewing body, we require the incorporation
of Regulation 18730 in the conflict of interest code. Local
agencies can only be urged to adopt it as the body of their
code. I have attached a memo which describes the regulation and
the procedure to follow to incorporate it into your code.

Based upon a cursory review of your conflict of interest
code, we have several concerns regarding the list of designated

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-90-225
Page 2


positions and their disclosure categories.  The Act requires that
designated employees be assigned a disclosure category which
requires the disclosure of those financial interests which the
employees could affect in their decision-making.  (Section
87302(a).)

     Designated positions whose duties are broad and indefinable
should be assigned disclosure covering all areas of reportable
interests.  For example, the Governing Board Members of your
agency would be assigned disclosure categories 1, 2 and 3 as
listed below:

     Category 1:    Persons in this category shall disclose any
real property in which the designated position has a direct or
indirect interest which is located within two miles of any land
purchased, leased, or used by the Agency.

     Category 2:    Persons in this category shall disclose all
business positions, investments in, or income (including gifts and
loans) received from business entities that manufacture, provide
or sell services and/or supplies, books, machinery, equipment,
provisions or other property of the type utilized by the agency.

     Category 3:    Persons in this category shall disclose
business positions, investments in, or income (including gifts &
loans) received from sources which are contractors or sub-
contractors engaged in the performance of work or services of the
type utilized by the Agency.

     Category 4:    Persons in this category shall disclose all
business positions, investments in, or income (including gifts and
loans) received from business entities that manufacture, provide
or sell services and/or supplies, books, machinery or equipment of
the type utilized by the Agency and associated with the job
assignment of designated positions assigned this disclosure
category.

     By utilizing the sample disclosure categories as listed
above, your list of designated positions would require
restructuring and should appear as follows:

     Governing Board Members                    1, 2 & 3
     Superintendent                             1, 2 & 3
     Deputy Superintendent                      1, 2 & 3
     Assistant Superintendent                   1, 2 & 3
     Assistant Superintendent                   1, 2 & 3
     Director of Purchasing and Stores          4
     Director of Food Services                  4
     Director of Maintenance & Operations       4
     Director of Special Programs               4
     Director of Curriculum                     4
     Consultants                                *

Our File No. I-90-225
Page 3


*Consultants shall be included in the list of designated
employees and shall disclose pursuant to the broadest
disclosure category in the code subject to the following
limitation:

The (chief executive officer) may determine in writing
that a particular consultant, although a "designated
position," is hired to perform a range of duties that is
limited in scope and thus is not required to fully comply
with the disclosure requirements in this section. Such
written determination shall include a description of the
consultant's duties and, based upon that description, a
statement of the extent of disclosure requirements. The
(chief executive officer's) determination is a public record
and shall be retained for public inspection in the same
manner and location as this conflict of interest code.

Your code at present does not provide for disclosure of
financial interests by consultants for the agency. The definition
of designated employee includes consultants. (Section 82019.)
A consultant is any natural person who provides under contract,
information, advice, recommendation or counsel to any agency.
(Regulation 18700.)

The Commission realizes that not all consultants participate
in the making of decisions on behalf of public agencies. Rather
than amend your code each time you retain a consultant that is in
a decision-making capacity, you may use a specialized disclosure
category that provides that the disclosure required of consultants
shall be determined on a case-by-case basis by the executive
officer for the district. Your executive officer may make a
determination as to what disclosure, if any, is required by any
particular consultant. Please note the consultant disclosure
category listed as footnote "*" above.

Upon review of the list of designated positions, we question
why the position of Assistant Superintendent for Business has been
given more extensive disclosure requirements than the Assistant
Superintendent for Admin. & Secondary Education. Perhaps both
Assistant Superintendent positions should be granted identical
disclosure category designation. If based upon job duty
statements for these two positions, you have determined that there
is a definite need to alter their disclosure pursuant to the types
of financial interests their decisions can affect, then you are
correct in assigning different disclosure requirements.

Our File No. I-90-225
Page 4


Please do not hesitate to contact me at (916) 322-5901 if you
should have questions regarding the above advice.

Sincerely,

Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel

By: Cheryl A. Hoff
Staff Services Analyst
Legal Division


KED:CAH:I90225

Enclosures

cc: John G. McLean,
Fair Political Practices Commission
Los Angeles County,
Board of Supervisors

sections.question_synthetic: What revisions are needed to the Palos Verdes Peninsula Unified School District's conflict of interest code to ensure compliance with the Political Reform Act and current FPPC standards?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises that the district should incorporate Regulation 18730 into its conflict of interest code, update its designated employee positions and disclosure categories to properly match employee duties (particularly for board members and administrative positions), and implement a case-by-case consultant disclosure determination process by the executive officer.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document lacks traditional QUESTION and CONCLUSION section headers but functions as an advice letter. Content includes specific regulatory guidance, sample disclosure categories, and recommended restructuring of designated positions. Some OCR artifacts present (e.g., 'rrasirr' for 'reform', 'ai.iquaf if ica-tion' for 'qualification'), but document content is substantially legible. Date: May 16, 1990.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What revisions are needed to the Palos Verdes Peninsula Unified School District's conflict of interest code to ensure compliance with the Political Reform Act and current FPPC standards?

CONCLUSION: The FPPC advises that the district should incorporate Regulation 18730 into its conflict of interest code, update its designated employee positions and disclosure categories to properly match employee duties (particularly for board members and administrative positions), and implement a case-by-case consultant disclosure determination process by the executive officer.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter to Palos Verdes Peninsula Unified School District identifying outdated provisions in their conflict of interest code and recommending incorporation of Regulation 18730, restructuring of designated positions with appropriate disclosure categories, and establishing a consultant disclosure category.
